question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker (mempool observer) front-run a victim's pending signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N} by submitting it first, burning nonce N, so the victim's own submission reverts with InvalidNonce, permanently blocking the operator from calling withdraw/repay on behalf of the victim until the victim signs a new message with nonce N+1? Preconditions: victim has signed an authorization with nonce N and broadcast it; attacker observes the mempool. Call sequence: attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) before victim; victim's tx reverts with InvalidNonce. Invariant tested: a valid signed authorization must not be consumable by an adversary in a way that permanently blocks the intended authorization state. Scoped impact: fund freeze — victim's operator cannot withdraw/repay until victim re-signs. Proof idea: fuzz nonce N, submit victim's signed tx from attacker address first, assert victim's subsequent tx reverts and operator cannot call withdraw(market, units, victim, receiver).

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized signer (isAuthorized[authorizer][signer]==true in Midnight) sign and submit Authorization{authorizer=authorizer, authorized=address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), isAuthorized=false, nonce=N} via EcrecoverAuthorizer, revoking EcrecoverAuthorizer's own delegation in Midnight, permanently blocking all future sig-based authorization changes for the authorizer? Preconditions: authorizer has previously called Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, authorizer); a second address signer is also authorized. Call sequence: signer signs auth with isAuthorized=false for authorized=ecrecoverAuthorizer; anyone submits it; Midnight.isAuthorized[authorizer][ecrecoverAuthorizer] becomes false; all future EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized calls for this authorizer revert with Unauthorized from Midnight. Invariant tested: an authorized signer must not be able to permanently revoke the EcrecoverAuthorizer's own delegation, freezing the sig-based path. Scoped impact: fund freeze — authorizer can no longer use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant/revoke operators needed for withdraw/repay. Proof idea: unit test — authorize signer, signer signs revocation of ecrecoverAuthorizer, submit, assert subsequent EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for authorizer reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized signer use EcrecoverAuthorizer to sign Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=signer, isAuthorized=true} granting themselves full Midnight authorization, then directly call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, false, victim) to revoke EcrecoverAuthorizer's delegation, locking the victim out of the sig-based path permanently? Preconditions: victim has authorized signer in Midnight (isAuthorized[victim][signer]==true); victim has also authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer. Call sequence: (1) signer signs auth granting themselves authorization via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) signer calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, false, victim) directly; (3) victim can no longer use EcrecoverAuthorizer. Invariant tested: coarse authorization must not allow an authorized signer to permanently block the victim's sig-based delegation path. Scoped impact: fund freeze — victim cannot use EcrecoverAuthorizer to manage operators for withdraw/repay. Proof idea: state test — verify that after step 2, EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for victim reverts with Unauthorized from Midnight.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker front-run a victim's signed Authorization{isAuthorized=false} revocation (intended to remove a malicious operator), causing the revocation to execute before the victim intended, leaving a window where the operator has already acted, and then the victim's re-authorization attempt uses a stale nonce? Preconditions: victim has signed a revocation for operator with nonce N; attacker observes mempool. Call sequence: attacker submits revocation first; operator's access is revoked; victim's tx reverts with InvalidNonce; victim must re-sign with nonce N+1. Invariant tested: front-running a revocation must not create a state where the victim's intended authorization sequence is disrupted in a way that freezes funds. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if victim is a smart contract that cannot re-sign, the authorization state is permanently stuck. Proof idea: fuzz deadline and nonce, simulate front-run, assert victim contract cannot recover authorization state.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Does EcrecoverAuthorizer use block.chainid (not Midnight's INITIAL_CHAIN_ID) in the domain separator, so that after a hard fork changing block.chainid, all pre-signed Authorization structs become permanently invalid, freezing any user who relied on pre-signed authorizations for operator-based withdraw/repay/liquidate flows? Preconditions: user has pre-signed authorizations with the pre-fork chainId; a hard fork changes block.chainid. Call sequence: post-fork, submit pre-signed authorization; ecrecover recovers wrong address; reverts with Unauthorized or InvalidSignature. Invariant tested: the domain separator must not silently invalidate all pre-signed authorizations in a way that permanently freezes operator-dependent fund flows. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all pre-signed operator authorizations become invalid post-fork. Proof idea: differential test — compute domain separator pre/post chainId change, assert ecrecover returns different addresses for same signature.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can a smart-contract authorizer (e.g., a multisig that can only produce EIP-712 signatures but cannot call Midnight directly) be permanently locked out of the EcrecoverAuthorizer path if its initial Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, authorizer) was never called, and the contract has no fallback to call Midnight directly? Preconditions: authorizer is a smart contract; it has never called Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, authorizer); it cannot call Midnight directly. Call sequence: any call to EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with authorizer as the authorizer reverts because Midnight.setIsAuthorized checks isAuthorized[authorizer][ecrecoverAuthorizer]==false. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer must not be the only path for a contract authorizer that cannot bootstrap its own Midnight authorization. Scoped impact: fund freeze — contract authorizer can never grant operators for withdraw/repay. Proof idea: unit test — deploy contract authorizer, attempt EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, assert revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized signer sign Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=maliciousOperator, isAuthorized=true} and then the maliciousOperator calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(victim_operator, false, victim) to revoke all of victim's legitimate operators, then calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, false, victim) to block the sig-based path, permanently freezing victim's ability to delegate withdraw/repay? Preconditions: victim has authorized signer in Midnight; signer is compromised or malicious. Call sequence: (1) signer signs auth for maliciousOperator; (2) maliciousOperator revokes all victim operators via Midnight.setIsAuthorized; (3) maliciousOperator revokes ecrecoverAuthorizer; (4) victim cannot withdraw or repay via any operator. Invariant tested: coarse authorization must not allow a chain of delegation to permanently freeze all exit paths. Scoped impact: fund freeze — victim cannot withdraw credit or repay debt via any operator. Proof idea: state test — execute full chain, assert victim.withdraw and victim.repay both revert with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit a victim's signed Authorization{isAuthorized=true, authorized=operator} at an unexpected time (e.g., after the victim has already revoked the operator via direct Midnight.setIsAuthorized), re-granting the operator access and enabling the operator to drain the victim's credit via withdraw? Preconditions: victim signed an authorization with nonce N but later revoked the operator directly; the signed message with nonce N is still valid (deadline not expired). Call sequence: attacker submits the old signed authorization; operator regains access; operator calls withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker). Invariant tested: a signed authorization must not be submittable after the authorizer has taken a direct action to revoke the operator. Scoped impact: fund freeze (or theft) — victim's credit is withdrawn by unauthorized operator. Proof idea: unit test — sign auth, revoke directly, submit old signed auth, assert operator can withdraw.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can ecrecover return a non-zero address for a signature with a high-s value (s > secp256k1n/2), allowing signature malleability where an attacker transforms a valid (v,r,s) into (v',r,s') with the same recovered address, submitting the transformed signature to burn the victim's nonce before the victim's original signature is submitted? Preconditions: victim has broadcast a signed authorization; attacker computes the malleable signature. Call sequence: attacker submits malleable signature; nonce increments; victim's original signature reverts with InvalidNonce. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer must reject malleable signatures (high-s) to prevent nonce griefing. Scoped impact: fund freeze — victim's nonce is burned, blocking intended authorization. Proof idea: unit test — compute malleable signature for a valid auth, submit it, assert nonce increments and original signature reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=victim, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N} signed by victim (or an authorized signer), causing Midnight.setIsAuthorized(victim, false, victim) to be called, which sets isAuthorized[victim][victim]=false, and does this have any downstream effect on withdraw/repay paths that check isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender] when msg.sender==onBehalf? Preconditions: victim has signed such an authorization; attacker submits it. Call sequence: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized called; Midnight.isAuthorized[victim][victim] set to false; victim calls withdraw(market, units, victim, victim) — passes because check is onBehalf==msg.sender. Invariant tested: self-deauthorization must not break the self-call path in withdraw/repay. Scoped impact: fund freeze — verify no path relies on isAuthorized[user][user] for self-calls. Proof idea: unit test — set isAuthorized[victim][victim]=false, assert victim can still call withdraw on their own behalf.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=address(0), isAuthorized=true} signed by victim, setting isAuthorized[victim][address(0)]=true in Midnight, and does this create any exploitable state where address(0) can be used as a payer or receiver in withdraw/repay/liquidate callbacks, causing fund freeze or loss? Preconditions: victim signs auth with authorized=address(0); attacker submits it. Call sequence: Midnight.isAuthorized[victim][address(0)] set to true; attacker attempts to exploit address(0) authorization in downstream calls. Invariant tested: authorizing address(0) must not create exploitable state in any withdraw/repay/liquidate path. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if any path uses isAuthorized[user][address(0)] as a bypass. Proof idea: unit test — set isAuthorized[victim][address(0)]=true, attempt all entry points with msg.sender=address(0), assert no bypass.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can a multicall sequence atomically (1) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to grant an operator, (2) have the operator perform a withdraw/repay, and (3) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to revoke the operator, all in one transaction, and does this create any state inconsistency or reentrancy issue that could freeze funds? Preconditions: victim has pre-signed both grant and revoke authorizations with consecutive nonces N and N+1. Call sequence: multicall([ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(grant), midnight.withdraw(...), ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(revoke)]). Invariant tested: multicall must not allow authorization state to be transiently set and unset in a way that bypasses per-action invariants or leaves accounting desynced. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if the revoke in step 3 fails, the operator retains permanent access. Proof idea: fuzz multicall ordering, assert authorization state is consistent after each step.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized signer sign Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=ecrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized=true} (re-authorizing ecrecoverAuthorizer) and simultaneously sign Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=ecrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized=false} with nonce N+1, then an attacker submits both in order, creating a race condition where the final state is revoked, permanently blocking the sig-based path? Preconditions: victim has authorized signer; signer signs both messages. Call sequence: submit grant (nonce N), submit revoke (nonce N+1); final state: isAuthorized[victim][ecrecoverAuthorizer]=false. Invariant tested: sequential nonce consumption must not allow an attacker to execute a grant-then-revoke sequence using pre-signed messages to freeze the sig-based path. Scoped impact: fund freeze — victim cannot use EcrecoverAuthorizer after the revoke. Proof idea: unit test — submit both signed messages in sequence, assert final state is revoked.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Does the EcrecoverAuthorizer correctly handle the case where authorization.authorizer == address(ecrecoverAuthorizer) itself, allowing someone to sign an authorization on behalf of the EcrecoverAuthorizer contract, and does this create any exploitable state in Midnight's isAuthorized mapping that could freeze downstream paths? Preconditions: attacker crafts Authorization{authorizer=address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true}; attacker signs it (ecrecover returns attacker's address, but check requires signer==authorizer or isAuthorized[ecrecoverAuthorizer][attacker]). Call sequence: attacker signs with their own key; signer==attacker != ecrecoverAuthorizer; check fails unless isAuthorized[ecrecoverAuthorizer][attacker]==true. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer must not be usable as an authorizer for itself in a way that creates circular authorization. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if circular authorization creates an unresolvable state. Proof idea: unit test — attempt to use ecrecoverAuthorizer as authorizer, assert correct revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who observes a victim's signed Authorization{isAuthorized=true, deadline=T} submit it at exactly block.timestamp==T (the deadline boundary), and does the <=  check in require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) correctly accept it, or does any off-by-one in block timestamp handling cause the authorization to be rejected, freezing the operator path? Preconditions: victim signs auth with deadline=T; block.timestamp==T at submission time. Call sequence: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized called at block.timestamp==T; require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) passes. Invariant tested: deadline boundary must be inclusive (<=) and consistent with block.timestamp semantics. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if deadline is incorrectly exclusive, valid authorizations near deadline are rejected. Proof idea: unit test — set deadline=block.timestamp, submit, assert success; set deadline=block.timestamp-1, assert Expired revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=true} with a valid signature from an address that was authorized in Midnight at signing time but has since been revoked (isAuthorized[victim][signer] is now false), and does EcrecoverAuthorizer check the authorization state at submission time (not signing time), causing a stale-authorization freeze? Preconditions: signer was authorized at signing time; victim revoked signer before submission; attacker submits the signed auth. Call sequence: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized called; signer check: isAuthorized[victim][signer] is now false; reverts with Unauthorized. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer must check authorization at submission time, not signing time, and this must not create a freeze where a valid-at-signing authorization is permanently rejected. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if victim revokes signer before submission, the authorization is permanently invalid even if the content was legitimate. Proof idea: unit test — sign auth, revoke signer, submit, assert Unauthorized revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by repeatedly submitting valid signed authorizations (grant then revoke, grant then revoke) using pre-signed messages with consecutive nonces, rapidly cycling the operator's authorization state and burning all pre-signed nonces, leaving the victim unable to use any pre-signed authorization and requiring them to sign new messages? Preconditions: victim has pre-signed N authorization messages with nonces 0..N-1; attacker has all signatures. Call sequence: attacker submits all N signed messages in sequence; all nonces consumed; victim's intended authorization state may be wrong (last message determines final state). Invariant tested: pre-signed authorization messages must not be consumable by an attacker in a sequence that leaves the victim's authorization state in an unintended frozen configuration. Scoped impact: fund freeze — victim's operators are in wrong state; victim must re-sign to recover. Proof idea: fuzz N, submit all pre-signed messages, assert final isAuthorized state matches last message's isAuthorized field.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker deploy a contract at a predictable address, pre-compute the EIP-712 domain separator for a future EcrecoverAuthorizer deployment at that address, and use signatures computed against that domain separator to authorize themselves before the contract is deployed, then exploit the authorization after deployment? Preconditions: attacker can predict the EcrecoverAuthorizer deployment address (e.g., via CREATE2 or nonce prediction). Call sequence: attacker pre-computes domain separator with future address; signs authorization; deploys EcrecoverAuthorizer at predicted address; submits pre-computed signature. Invariant tested: domain separator must bind to the actual deployed contract address, preventing pre-deployment signature attacks. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if attacker can pre-authorize themselves, they can revoke victim's operators post-deployment. Proof idea: unit test — pre-compute domain separator, deploy at predicted address, submit pre-signed auth, assert it succeeds or fails correctly.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized signer sign Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=repayOperator, isAuthorized=false} to revoke the repay operator just before a debt becomes due at maturity, causing the victim's debt to go unrepaid, triggering post-maturity liquidation of a position that would otherwise have been repaid? Preconditions: victim has authorized signer and repayOperator; signer is malicious; victim's debt is due at maturity. Call sequence: signer signs revocation of repayOperator; anyone submits it; repayOperator cannot call repay(market, units, victim, ...); maturity passes; victim's position is liquidated. Invariant tested: coarse authorization must not allow an authorized signer to block the repay path, causing forced liquidation. Scoped impact: fund freeze/loss — victim's collateral is seized in liquidation that could have been avoided. Proof idea: state test — revoke repayOperator before maturity, warp to maturity+1, assert liquidation succeeds and victim loses collateral.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized signer sign Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=liquidatorOperator, isAuthorized=false} to revoke the liquidator's authorization, preventing the liquidator from calling liquidate on behalf of the victim (if the victim is the liquidator's onBehalf), and does this create a scenario where an unhealthy position cannot be liquidated? Preconditions: liquidation requires the liquidator to be authorized by the borrower (e.g., via a liquidator gate that checks authorization); signer revokes liquidator. Call sequence: signer revokes liquidatorOperator; liquidator cannot liquidate victim's position; position remains unhealthy and unliquidated. Invariant tested: authorization revocation must not block the liquidation path for unhealthy positions. Scoped impact: fund freeze — unhealthy position cannot be liquidated, bad debt accumulates. Proof idea: state test — revoke liquidator authorization, attempt liquidate, assert revert or success depending on gate configuration.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=withdrawOperator, isAuthorized=true} with a valid signature, then immediately in the same block submit Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=withdrawOperator, isAuthorized=false} with nonce N+1 (if they have both pre-signed messages), atomically granting and revoking the operator, and does this leave any partial state in Midnight that could freeze funds? Preconditions: victim has pre-signed both messages; attacker has both signatures. Call sequence: submit grant (nonce N) then revoke (nonce N+1) in same block; final state: isAuthorized[victim][withdrawOperator]=false. Invariant tested: atomic grant-revoke must not leave any intermediate state in Midnight that persists and causes fund freeze. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if any intermediate state (e.g., partial credit update) persists. Proof idea: unit test — submit both in same block, assert final state is revoked and no intermediate state persists.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Does the nonce[authorization.authorizer]++ increment in EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized use checked arithmetic (Solidity 0.8.34 default), meaning that if nonce[authorizer] reaches type(uint256).max, the next call reverts with arithmetic overflow, permanently blocking the EcrecoverAuthorizer path for that authorizer? Preconditions: nonce[authorizer] == type(uint256).max (requires type(uint256).max successful calls, practically impossible, but worth testing for correctness). Call sequence: attempt EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized when nonce is at max; arithmetic overflow reverts. Invariant tested: nonce overflow must not permanently freeze the EcrecoverAuthorizer path. Scoped impact: fund freeze — authorizer can never use EcrecoverAuthorizer again after nonce overflow. Proof idea: unit test — set nonce to type(uint256).max via storage manipulation, attempt setIsAuthorized, assert overflow revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use a flashLoan callback to atomically (1) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to grant themselves authorization over a victim, (2) call withdraw/repay on behalf of the victim, and (3) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to revoke their own authorization, all within the flashLoan callback, exploiting the permissionless submission of signed authorizations? Preconditions: victim has pre-signed authorization messages; attacker has the signatures; attacker initiates a flashLoan. Call sequence: flashLoan callback calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(grant), midnight.withdraw(victim), ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(revoke). Invariant tested: flashLoan callbacks must not be usable to transiently grant and use authorization in a way that bypasses fund protection. Scoped impact: fund freeze or theft — victim's funds withdrawn within flashLoan callback. Proof idea: PoC — implement flashLoan callback with the above sequence, assert victim's credit is drained.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=feeClaimerOperator, isAuthorized=false} to revoke the fee claimer's authorization, preventing the fee claimer from calling claimSettlementFee or claimContinuousFee on behalf of the victim, causing fees to accumulate unclaimed and eventually become unclaimable if the fee claimer role changes? Preconditions: victim has authorized feeClaimerOperator; signer (authorized by victim) signs revocation. Call sequence: revocation submitted; feeClaimerOperator cannot claim fees on behalf of victim; fees accumulate; fee claimer role changes; old fees become unclaimable. Invariant tested: authorization revocation must not permanently block the fee claim path. Scoped impact: fund freeze — accrued fees cannot be claimed. Proof idea: state test — revoke fee claimer operator, attempt claimContinuousFee, assert revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Permanent or long-term freezing of user funds caused by broken withdraw, repay, liquidate, claim fee, or settlement paths'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker craft a signature with v=0 or v=1 (non-standard values) that causes ecrecover to return address(0), which is caught by the signer != address(0) check, but does any edge case in the v value handling allow ecrecover to return a non-zero address that matches an existing authorized address, bypassing the signature check? Preconditions: attacker crafts signature with non-standard v value. Call sequence: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized called with crafted signature; ecrecover behavior with v=0,1 tested. Invariant tested: ecrecover must not return a valid non-zero address for non-standard v values that could match an authorized address. Scoped impact: fund freeze — if attacker can forge authorization, they can revoke victim's operators. Proof idea: fuzz v in {0,1,2,...,255}, assert ecrecover returns address(0) for all non-standard values.
